title: Testing, Fault Injection, Release, and Production Defense · Guided Systems Studio
summary: A delivery team incrementally builds production file-delivery defense dossier, changes one file or failure, and keeps earlier HTTP, Go, accessibility, security, and resource-budget invariants active. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of production file-delivery defense dossier while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Use table, property, and fuzz tests for key, range, policy, lifecycle, state-machine, checksum, retry, and cache invariants with changed cases.
Use fakes and emulators for deterministic feedback while listing unsupported AWS semantics and assigning controlled integration tests.
Gate formatting, vet, static analysis, tests, fuzz seeds, race, goroutine and body leaks, benchmarks, profiles, reproducible build, and dependency review.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Commit to the mission model · Workshop Pure Contract Property Tests
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop Go file-delivery team handles case 9436 during a high-traffic evidence intake window. Build deterministic Go evidence to gate release on deterministic, Go runtime, controlled AWS, fault, load, restore, and rollback evidence; do not treat an ETag, CORS result, emulator, or successful SDK call as stronger proof than it is; then cancel halfway through and prove every body, part, and goroutine reaches a terminal state. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: S3, CloudFront, and Go Commit to the mission model · Workshop Pure Contract Property Tests.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for use table, property, and fuzz tests for key, range, policy, lifecycle, state-machine, checksum, retry, and cache invariants with changed cases. For S3, CloudFront, and Go Commit to the mission model · Workshop Pure Contract Property Tests, keep Pure Contract Property Tests connected to executable Go storage and delivery decision evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop Compatible Release Defense
steps.instruction:
Append a compile-ready pure-Go function headed "// Evidence: storage-go-compatible-release-defense-183w6c" that uses case 7953 to gate release on deterministic, Go runtime, controlled AWS, fault, load, restore, and rollback evidence. Return observable changed-case evidence. Browser code must not import AWS SDK packages, open sockets, contact S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, networks, databases, or external effects, read host state, execute host commands, or use credentials; verify those boundaries later with Go 1.26.5 compile, vet, fuzz, race, leak, controlled AWS, load, fault, security, cost, restore, and production gates.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Compatible Release Defense
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop Go file-delivery team handles case 5484 during a regional dependency incident. Build deterministic Go evidence to gate release on deterministic, Go runtime, controlled AWS, fault, load, restore, and rollback evidence; keep browser execution deterministic and move AWS claims to an authorized transfer gate; then cancel halfway through and prove every body, part, and goroutine reaches a terminal state. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Canary immutable artifacts and infrastructure, compare changed behavior and cost, preserve rollback data and policies, rehearse recovery, and defend residual risk.

One precondition fails while output still looks plausible. What is the correct response?
